design_philosophy.core_loop: Raise/lower terrain to create buildable land → followers build houses → population grows → mana accumulates → unlock divine powers → use powers to reshape world and eliminate enemy followers → progress to next level
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Population growth vs. enemy expansion; resource management of mana; landscape manipulation as both construction and destruction tool
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a deity who shapes civilization through divine intervention, building up followers while destroying enemy forces through god-like powers
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneering god game that treats landscape manipulation as the primary verb rather than direct unit control. Player is omnipotent architect rather than commander. Core design emerged from solving a technical problem (helping peeps navigate terrain) into a core mechanic. The game's controversial design decision: no traditional RTS combat units—only divine powers and follower population serve as weapons.

difficulty.continues_system: Level-based progression with 500 levels to complete
difficulty.checkpointing: Each level is discrete; failure requires restarting that level
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player can choose order of divine power usage, terrain shaping strategy, and pacing. Landscape type (desert, snow, lava, etc.) affects both player and enemy follower development, creating asymmetric challenges across levels

progression.upgrade_system: Mana-gated divine powers unlocked as population grows. Powers include: raise/lower land (base), earthquakes, floods, swamps, volcanoes, knight conversion (upgrade followers to knights)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Mana meter fills as followers build houses and expand population. Each power tier requires mana threshold. Landscape type varies per level, providing new environmental challenges
replayability_hooks: 500 discrete levels with varied landscapes (desert, rock/lava, snow/ice)
Different optimal strategies per landscape type
Asymmetric enemy power scaling based on terrain
Multiplayer mode allowing competitive deity battles

world_building_techniques: Isometric 'tabletop' aesthetic frames world as game-within-game
Book metaphor: world depicted as open book pages, suggesting divine observation
Landscape variety (biomes) provides visual world-building without narrative
Deity vs. deity framing provides implicit narrative (conflict of wills)

faction_indicators: Player deity vs. opposing deity (binary faction split)
Follower population as faction strength metric
Landscape control as territorial dominance indicator
danger_signals: Loss of buildable land to enemy construction
environmental_storytelling: Landscape transformation reflects divine struggle (raised vs. lowered terrain)
Follower settlement patterns indicate population pressure
Crater/volcanic damage from divine powers shows intervention history
Flooded areas mark failed territories or divine punishment
